./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/claude-sonnet-5.md:214:      (c) adversarial contributors flood the federation with
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/claude-sonnet-5.md:221:    federated commons. Who are the REAL existing systems that
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/claude-sonnet-5.md:226:      - Mastodon (federated social, no semantic graph)
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/claude-sonnet-5.md:231:      - Bluesky / AT Protocol (federated identity + content)
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/claude-sonnet-5.md:247:  - For Q3: a proposed benchmark for measuring federation-level
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/claude-sonnet-5.md:273:The stack is TypeScript / DDD with clean layer separation (`domain` → `application` → `infrastructure` → `cli`/`mcp`). Domain layer is pure: immutable graph values, `neverthrow` Result monads throughout, no I/O. Key domain types confirm what the prompt describes: `graph.ts` (immutable GraphNode/GraphEdge), `eval-metrics.ts` (pure `recallAtK`/`ndcgAtK`/`reciprocalRank`), `contextual-enrich.ts` (E11, pure, deterministic), `llm-listwise-rerank.ts` (ports-and-adapters for LLM, shuffle already shipped at commit 1f828b7), `sharing.ts` (SEC-03 boundary: no raw embeddings cross the wire), `oracle.ts` (P2P Q&A as pure graph-node data shape), `federated-search.ts` (Promise.all fan-out with 2000ms per-peer timeout, dedup prefers local). The architecture is sound for the Akashik mission. The retrieval plumbing is well-built. The open question is what to measure next.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/claude-sonnet-5.md:281:Here is why. LME-S shows R@50 = 1.0 — the gold is always in the candidate pool; the ceiling is real. The remaining ~4pp NDCG@5 headroom is the gap between ranks 1 and 2-5. On LoCoMo the headroom is 60pp, but the only interventions that produced lift were write-path ones (E11), not read-path ones. Small local LLMs actively regression on LoCoMo after shuffle.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/claude-sonnet-5.md:285:**The tradeoff of stopping retrieval tuning:** You leave 60pp of theoretical LoCoMo headroom on the table. However, that headroom requires a larger model than what runs locally (qwen2.5:7b already regressed). Capturing it requires a cloud API call, which reintroduces network dependency and cost per query — counter to the local-first architecture. The tradeoff is: spend a week definitively answering whether a cloud API call on LoCoMo lifts R@3 above bi-encoder (one 50-question spot-check with GPT-4o-mini would settle this), then either ship the cloud reranker as an opt-in tier or close the listwise chapter entirely.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/claude-sonnet-5.md:301:| System | What it shares with Akashik | What's missing |
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/claude-sonnet-5.md:305:| **W3C SPARQL 1.1 Federated Queries** (2013 spec) | Semantic federation: one query fans out to multiple SPARQL endpoints, results merged. | Requires central service-description registry for peer discovery. No P2P pull. No attribution. No caching compounding. |
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/claude-sonnet-5.md:317:No existing public benchmark measures federated knowledge compounding. This is a real gap and a publishable contribution.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/claude-sonnet-5.md:335:| `R_federated@k` vs `R_local@k` | Head-to-head on the same query set | Federation lift over single-peer |
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/claude-sonnet-5.md:337:**The key experimental design decision:** corpora must be strictly disjoint at setup time, with controlled overlap introduced as a variable. This lets you separate "federation lift from serendipitous overlap" from "genuine cross-peer knowledge transfer."
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/claude-sonnet-5.md:339:**Baseline condition:** all queries evaluated local-only (federation disabled). The delta is the federation value.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/claude-sonnet-5.md:357:- **Availability confounding.** Your current bench runs with all peers online. In production, the 2000ms timeout means some peers never respond. Your single-peer benchmarks (LME-S, LoCoMo) don't model this. AkashikBench-F should include a "partial availability" condition: what is federation R@k when 20% of peers are offline?
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/claude-sonnet-5.md:359:- **Corpus contamination.** If you run multi-peer simulation with overlapping source corpora (e.g., two peers that both indexed the same web page), the federation looks better than it is — you're measuring deduplication handling, not genuine knowledge transfer. Strict corpus disjointness at setup is non-negotiable for valid results.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/claude-sonnet-5.md:375:**The real risk:** publishing "akashik 0.9202 vs mem0 0.925" when the evaluation protocols differ is a credibility trap. The correct framing for Akashik launch is NOT R@5 comparison against single-user products. It's `web_fallback_rate` vs baseline (before federation) — a metric only Akashik can report.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/claude-sonnet-5.md:395:Instrument and publish `web_fallback_rate` for the `rust` room. The expected curve: day 1 ≈ 80-90% web fallback (sparse graph), day 30 < 40% (librarian + early-adopter knowledge covers the common queries). This is the compounding claim made visible.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/claude-sonnet-5.md:407:The known solution is **topic diversity indexing**, analogous to Mastodon's local vs. federated timeline split. Akashik's room structure partially mitigates this (niche rooms exist independently), but within a room, hot nodes will crowd out cold ones in search rankings.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/claude-sonnet-5.md:409:Specific mitigation: weight the federation fan-out ordering by inverse document frequency across rooms. Nodes that exist on only one peer get a boost in ranking — scarcity is a signal for uniqueness, not irrelevance. The `peer-reputation-store.ts` infrastructure already exists; extend it to track per-topic peer coverage as a first-class signal.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/claude-sonnet-5.md:415:The `hot-cache.ts` and `hot-cache-tick.ts` files already exist in the codebase. The current hot-cache policy appears to cache popular items. The fix is to extend the policy to include **uniqueness** as a caching criterion: if a node exists on only one online peer in its room, any peer that queries it should cache it even if it's not popular. This is the BitTorrent seeding-ratio model applied to knowledge nodes.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/claude-sonnet-5.md:419:This is the hardest problem and every federated system has some version of it.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/claude-sonnet-5.md:433:This is the most important one. AT Protocol solved federated identity (DIDs), signed content, Personal Data Servers (each user owns their data), and protocol-level federation at scale (millions of users). Their architecture maps almost directly: PDS = Akashik peer, DID = Akashik contributor identity, lexicon record type = Akashik graph node type.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/claude-sonnet-5.md:457:> "This is a coordination problem in an infrastructure costume. Compounding is a property of participation density, not architecture. The mechanism only works when enough peers are online and have already pulled the relevant knowledge — which requires network effects you haven't earned yet. Every federated network (Mastodon, Matrix, Diaspora) has built technically correct architecture and then struggled for years to reach the critical mass where the federated property is actually better than the centralized alternative. Akashik's architectural insight about R(T,t) is correct but irrelevant until you have R(T, t) >> 0 for enough T, which is a community-building problem, not an engineering problem. The next engineering month could be zero effort and the project would succeed faster by spending that month on community seeding and distribution."
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/claude-sonnet-5.md:463:The cold-start problem is real for every network, but it conflates two distinct claims. The first claim — "federation is valuable once you have density" — is trivially true and grants the counter-argument. The second claim — "Akashik provides value before density" — is the actual product bet, and it's defensible: the web-fallback path means a contributor with zero peers still gets a functional local knowledge tool on day 1. Federation value is strictly additive. The counter-argument applies if Akashik required federation to work at all; it doesn't. However, the counter-argument correctly identifies the real risk: if the compounding claim is made at launch but can't be demonstrated (because the network is too sparse), early adopters experience a tool that feels like a slower version of their existing web search. The engineering month is not zero precisely because without `web_fallback_rate` instrumentation and AkashikBench-F, there is no number to show at launch that makes the compounding claim concrete rather than aspirational. The engineering constraint is measurement, not architecture.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/claude-sonnet-5.md:471:- **Read-path reranking with local LLMs does not beat bi-encoder** on either benchmark, even after fixing the shuffle bias. This is not a bug in the implementation — the implementation is correct. The small-model listwise chapter is empirically closed unless a cloud-API ablation shows the headroom is capturable at all.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/claude-sonnet-5.md:472:- **The identity pivot is architecturally correct.** The codebase already has all the infrastructure needed for the Akashik mission: federated search with dedup and attribution (`federated-search.ts`), peer reputation (`peer-reputation-store.ts`), oracle P2P Q&A (`oracle.ts`), DID identity (`identity-store.ts`), SEC-03 metadata boundary (`sharing.ts`). None of this needs to be built. What needs to be built is the measurement infrastructure for what the mission claims.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/claude-sonnet-5.md:478:2. **`web_fallback_rate` as a first-class production metric** — instrument it per room in the telemetry pipeline (`telemetry-formatter.ts` already exists). This is the launch number.

./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-6-2026-05-26/probes/codex-probe-0.md:228:3. A fundamental pivot from 'akashik: agent memory product' to 'Akashik: federated knowledge commons for the OSS community'
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-6-2026-05-26/probes/codex-probe-0.md:229:4. The articulation of the compounding mechanism (peer-local + federation-on-query + web-on-miss + save-locally + transfer-on-next-ask)

./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-6-2026-05-26/probes/codex-probe-0.md:903:The file map is clear now: there’s a live Akashik/P2P/federation track sitting beside a large amount of legacy retrieval-optimization and phase-era planning material. I’m reading representative files next so the keep/archive/delete calls are based on actual content, not just filenames.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-6-2026-05-26/probes/codex-probe-0.md:911:Akashik is a peer-to-peer knowledge graph protocol where every researcher, maintainer, and engineer adds their reading and reasoning, and where every newcomer can query what the community has already learned before re-treading the same path. Each contribution is Ed25519-signed, locally owned, and federated only on demand — so the network's working set grows by what its contributors are actually curious about, not by what a central planner decided to ingest.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-6-2026-05-26/probes/codex-probe-0.md:917:Open source built the freest software stack in history because we shared *code* — CRAN, npm, PyPI, GitHub, arXiv. The infrastructure compounds. But the knowledge *between* the code (what we read, what we figured out together, what we debugged at 3am) has never had the same substrate. Each generation of contributors starts over. The same papers get re-explained in posts that 404 within a year. The same CUDA OOM gets diagnosed in parallel by fifty people next Tuesday.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-6-2026-05-26/probes/codex-probe-0.md:919:**Akashik is what** that missing substrate looks like: a federated, cryptographically-attested record the community writes for itself. Not a personal-memory product. Not a team wiki. A shared protocol where contributor reading-hours compound into community progress, signed and attributed, forever.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-6-2026-05-26/probes/codex-probe-0.md:921:**The differentiator is federation, not retrieval.** Single-user memory products already solve personal retrieval; Akashik is not trying to beat them on per-user R@5. The bet is that *cross-peer transfer* of researched-once knowledge is the missing primitive in the OSS knowledge stack, and that a demand-shaped P2P graph is the right way to build it.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-6-2026-05-26/probes/codex-probe-0.md:923:The name borrows from the mythological **Akashic Records** — the perfect referent because it names what we're building: a shared, persistent, accessible record of what the collective has known. The "k" instead of "c" signals the engineering implementation, not the literal myth.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-6-2026-05-26/probes/codex-probe-0.md:932:2. **Federation on miss.** Fan out to the peers you share rooms with. Each answers with whatever they've already saved or researched. Results merge via reciprocal-rank fusion.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-6-2026-05-26/probes/codex-probe-0.md:933:3. **Web on second miss.** If the federation can't answer with confidence, the harness performs `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` / arXiv pull on *your* machine — the only time the network reaches outward.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-6-2026-05-26/probes/codex-probe-0.md:934:4. **Save locally, signed.** The result lands in your local graph, attested by your DID (Ed25519). You are now the "ambitioned" curator of that knowledge: provenance, room, timestamp, source URLs all attached.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-6-2026-05-26/probes/codex-probe-0.md:935:5. **Transfer on next ask.** When another contributor asks something similar later, federation fan-out reaches your peer, your research transfers to them with original attribution, and they pay nothing for the hour you spent.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-6-2026-05-26/probes/codex-probe-0.md:951:The Round 5 octopus-discover synthesis identified one benchmark capable of falsifying the federated-commons thesis: a federation-level simulator measuring `web_fallback_rate(t)` over a realistic peer network with offline churn. We built it. First run, on the LoCoMo factual subset:
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-6-2026-05-26/probes/codex-probe-0.md:970:| Web fallback | 4.5% | Outbound only when federation couldn't answer |
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-6-2026-05-26/probes/codex-probe-0.md:974:Bench source: [`tests/bench-akashik-federation.test.ts`](tests/bench-akashik-federation.test.ts).
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-6-2026-05-26/probes/codex-probe-0.md:999:akashik save https://arxiv.org/abs/2406.16678 --room research
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-6-2026-05-26/probes/codex-probe-0.md:1000:akashik save ./notes/cuda-oom-debug.md --room toolshed
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-6-2026-05-26/probes/codex-probe-0.md:1009:The query checks your local graph, then federates to peers you share rooms with, then falls back to web research only if neither can answer. The web result lands in your local graph signed by you — the next contributor who asks something similar pulls it from your peer with full attribution.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-6-2026-05-26/probes/codex-probe-0.md:1017:- **P2P federation over libp2p.** No central server, no vendor data lock-in. Each peer advertises the rooms it participates in; queries fan out via gossip with bounded timeouts. Lineage per the Round 5 synthesis: *Freenet-style demand-shaped lazy replication with cache-fill on miss*, applied to attributed semantic memory.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-6-2026-05-26/probes/codex-probe-0.md:1018:- **Ed25519-signed contributions with DIDs.** Every record carries the curator's decentralized identifier, room, source URLs, and timestamp. Trust is graph-traversable: follow the chain, see who curated, when, and why. Lineage: AT Protocol's signed-attribution layer applied to research memory rather than social posts.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-6-2026-05-26/probes/codex-probe-0.md:1034:- **Rarity-aware replication quotas** — LOCKSS-style protection against niche-content evaporation; BitTorrent rarest-first weighting on federation fan-out.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-6-2026-05-26/probes/codex-probe-0.md:1040:Akashik is pre-launch — the federation simulator validates the thesis, the retrieval stack benchmarks at parity with public single-user baselines, and the real pilot is the next milestone. We need:

./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-6-2026-05-26/probes/codex-probe-0.md:1587:| Per-room private CRDTs (Roam-style multiplayer) | Closed by default; doesn't compound across rooms; no path to community-wide commons |

./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-6-2026-05-26/probes/codex-probe-0.md:1991:Beyond that: **federated retrieval** (the unique Akashik bet) — measure how P2P-shared rooms across multiple peers lift recall on out-of-distribution questions, vs single-peer baselines. No public benchmark covers this today; we'd publish one.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-6-2026-05-26/probes/codex-probe-0.md:2004:| **E11** | **Rule-based contextual enrichment (write-path)** | Prepend structured metadata (date, room/persona, participants, top-K extracted entities) to each session's text *before* embedding. Like Anthropic's Contextual Retrieval but rule-based — no LLM call, zero ingest cost beyond regex/NER. | multi-session **10pp**, temporal **13pp** | +3–5pp aggregate | 3-4 h |
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-6-2026-05-26/probes/codex-probe-0.md:2144:LongMemEval-S specifically has +5 pp of headroom (R@5 0.92 vs R@20 0.99) that the cross-encoder demonstrably failed to capture with bge-reranker-base. The LLM listwise has a structural reason to succeed where bge failed: it can use the temporal-reasoning capability the cross-encoder lacks, and that's exactly the question type sitting in the gold-tail.

./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-6-2026-05-26/probes/codex-probe-0.md:5823:**v2.0 makes them connected.** A peer-to-peer network where akashik nodes discover each other, share graph fragments, and build a collective knowledge layer that's bigger than any single user's research.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-6-2026-05-26/probes/codex-probe-0.md:5829:     │ 3 rooms  │                  │ 2 rooms  │
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-6-2026-05-26/probes/codex-probe-0.md:5849:- Each node exposes a subset of its graph as "public rooms"
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-6-2026-05-26/probes/codex-probe-0.md:5850:- Private rooms stay local (homelab stays mine)
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-6-2026-05-26/probes/codex-probe-0.md:5851:- Shared rooms are replicated via CRDT (conflict-free replicated data types)
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-6-2026-05-26/probes/codex-probe-0.md:5852:- Only node metadata + embeddings are shared — not raw source content
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-6-2026-05-26/probes/codex-probe-0.md:5860:- **Opt-in only** — nothing shared by default
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-6-2026-05-26/probes/codex-probe-0.md:5861:- **Room-level control** — mark rooms as public/private
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-6-2026-05-26/probes/codex-probe-0.md:5862:- **Metadata only** — share node labels + embeddings, not full text

./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-6-2026-05-26/probes/codex-probe-0.md:5897:Use **Automerge** or **Y.js** for the room-level CRDT:
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-6-2026-05-26/probes/codex-probe-0.md:5898:- Each shared room is a CRDT document
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-6-2026-05-26/probes/codex-probe-0.md:5909:A project maintainer shares their `project-x` room publicly. Contributors connect as peers and get the maintainer's research context (relevant papers, HN discussions, competitor analysis) automatically merged into their local graph.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-6-2026-05-26/probes/codex-probe-0.md:5922:- Public/private room marking (`share room/unshare room`)
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-6-2026-05-26/probes/codex-probe-0.md:5923:- CRDT-based room sync (Automerge)
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-6-2026-05-26/probes/codex-probe-0.md:5941:This is the end state: **a decentralized knowledge graph where every coding agent in the world shares what it learned.**

./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-5-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:12:Akashik = federated knowledge commons for the OSS community.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-5-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:14:  local-first → federation-fan-out → web-on-miss → save-locally
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-5-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:22:  LoCoMo R@3 = 0.392, R@30 = 0.993 — 60pp rerank headroom unmined
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-5-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:34:     federation rather than per-peer retrieval?
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-5-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:44:Q3 — There is no public benchmark for federation-level
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-5-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:95:1. **Q1**: Spend the next engineering month on **architecting federation**, not chasing `R@5` marketing gains. Your own numbers say the head is already close to saturated on LME-S (`R@5=0.9202`) while cross-encoder reranking was a clean null and small-LLM listwise underperformed the bi-encoder; that is exactly the profile of a system where another few benchmark points are possible, but strategically weak. The differentiator is the federation-level loop: local-first ownership, miss-triggered acquisition, and cross-peer reuse. The upside is a real moat and a product claim nobody else in agent memory currently owns; the downside is higher systems risk around provenance, abuse, deletion/tombstones, and auditability, which you should treat as first-class now rather than as launch debt.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-5-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:99:3. **Q3**: Build a benchmark I’d call **FedComp-OSS**. Use a frozen snapshot of 6-8 OSS ecosystems with real recurring knowledge demand: `aider`, `Continue`, `OpenHands`, `LangGraph`, `Haystack`, `bluesky-social/atproto`, `holepunchto/hypercore`, and `astral-sh/ruff`. Source documents are docs, issues, PRs, release notes, ADRs, and benchmark papers; question generation can borrow the “community Q/A over technical corpora” spirit of **FreshStack** (arXiv:2504.13128, https://arxiv.org/abs/2504.13128) and repo-level QA framing from **CodeRepoQA** (arXiv:2412.14764, https://arxiv.org/abs/2412.14764). Simulate `64` peers, each with 1-2 affinity communities and only a local subset of artifacts; queries arrive in a Zipfian stream with head and long-tail overlap. On each miss: local search -> federate to `k` peers -> frozen web corpus on miss -> cache locally -> optionally transfer to later peers when curator is “online.” Measure `Answer@k`, local-hit rate, federation lift over isolated peers, miss-to-future-hit conversion, unique artifact coverage, transfer cost, and diversity (`Gini`/entropy of retained records). Define **compounding** quantitatively as the area-under-curve improvement of local hit rate over time versus a no-sharing control, plus `MissToFutureHit@7d`: the fraction of misses that become hits for a different peer within 7 simulated days. This is runnable in a week on commodity hardware with `SQLite + FAISS` and a small embedding model such as `bge-small-en-v1.5`; however, the tradeoff is lower realism than a live network, so freeze the corpus and publish the simulator.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-5-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:106:5. **Q5**: The smallest viable 30-day launch is **one narrow wedge: agent-memory and coding-agent OSS**. Seed five named communities with about 20 contributors each: `aider`, `Continue`, `OpenHands`, `LangGraph`, and `Haystack`. Seed content should be concrete and already debated there: **LongMemEval** (arXiv:2410.10813, https://arxiv.org/abs/2410.10813), **LoCoMo** (arXiv:2402.17753, https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.17753), **Mem0** (arXiv:2504.19413, https://arxiv.org/abs/2504.19413; repo: https://github.com/mem0ai/mem0), **Agent Memory Benchmark** (https://agentmemorybenchmark.ai/), **FreshStack** (arXiv:2504.13128), and one federation substrate pair: `bluesky-social/atproto` (https://github.com/bluesky-social/atproto) and `holepunchto/hypercore` (https://github.com/holepunchto/hypercore). Timeline: week 1 seed 50-80 canonical artifacts and instrument miss/hit/provenance logs; week 2 recruit 25 design partners from those communities; week 3 run “ask-reuse-transfer” challenges and publish daily compounding graphs; week 4 ship a public writeup showing at least one concrete case where knowledge found by one contributor becomes a local hit for another. The upside is visible overlap fast; conversely, if you spread across unrelated OSS communities, you will get vanity signups without observable compounding.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-5-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:114:- **agentmemory**: strongest comparison on local retrieval quality; Akashik should position as “everything agentmemory does locally, plus cross-peer transfer and federation-level compounding.” Source: https://www.agentmemory.tech/
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-5-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:115:- **mem0**: strong comparison on production memory infrastructure; Akashik position is “peer-owned and federated memory commons, not an app-scoped memory layer.” Source: https://github.com/mem0ai/mem0 and arXiv:2504.19413.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-5-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:116:- **Bluesky / AT Protocol**: strongest comparison on federation architecture; Akashik position is “ATProto for knowledge artifacts and demand-shaped retrieval, not social posting.” Sources: https://github.com/bluesky-social/atproto and https://atproto.com/guides/overview
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-5-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:118:8. **Q8**: The devastating argument is: **“This is an overengineered federation story searching for a workload. Retrieval is already near-solved locally, cross-user query overlap is probably too low to create real compounding, and the moment you add federation you inherit abuse, provenance, privacy, deletion, and trust problems that centralized copilots or per-user memory systems avoid.”** The honest response is that this critique is valid unless Akashik proves a narrow, high-overlap use case quickly. So the burden of proof is not philosophical decentralization; it is a measurable federation lift in one community, with signed provenance, moderation/audit logs, and deletion/tombstone semantics good enough that the knowledge graph does not become an ungovernable exfiltration or misinformation cache. If the 30-day pilot cannot show repeated miss-to-hit conversion across different users, the project should be narrowed to local-first memory sync rather than a federated commons.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-5-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:120:If I were Sahar, the next engineering month I’d spend on **federation architecture** because that is the only part that creates a defensible product difference rather than another benchmark decimal. The next marketing/launch month I’d spend on **a 100-person pilot across aider, Continue, OpenHands, LangGraph, and Haystack** because those communities have overlapping questions and enough activity to make compounding visible fast. Specifically NOT **chasing `R@5` from `0.9202` toward `0.952` as the primary goal** because the upside is mostly narrative polish while the unresolved risk is whether cross-peer compounding exists at all.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-5-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:181:Akashik = federated knowledge commons for the OSS community.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-5-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:183:  local-first → federation-fan-out → web-on-miss → save-locally

Your own numbers say the head is already close to saturated on LME-S (`R@5=0.9202`) while cross-encoder reranking was a clean null and small-LLM listwise underperformed the bi-encoder; that is exactly the profile of a system where another few benchmark points are possible, but strategically weak. The differentiator is the federation-level loop: local-first ownership, miss-triggered acquisition, and cross-peer reuse. The upside is a real moat and a product claim nobody else in agent memory currently owns; the downside is higher systems risk around provenance, abuse, deletion/tombstones, and auditability, which you should treat as first-class now rather than as launch debt.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-5-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:322:3. **Q3**: Build a benchmark I’d call **FedComp-OSS**. Use a frozen snapshot of 6-8 OSS ecosystems with real recurring knowledge demand: `aider`, `Continue`, `OpenHands`, `LangGraph`, `Haystack`, `bluesky-social/atproto`, `holepunchto/hypercore`, and `astral-sh/ruff`. Source documents are docs, issues, PRs, release notes, ADRs, and benchmark papers; question generation can borrow the “community Q/A over technical corpora” spirit of **FreshStack** (arXiv:2504.13128, https://arxiv.org/abs/2504.13128) and repo-level QA framing from **CodeRepoQA** (arXiv:2412.14764, https://arxiv.org/abs/2412.14764). Simulate `64` peers, each with 1-2 affinity communities and only a local subset of artifacts; queries arrive in a Zipfian stream with head and long-tail overlap. On each miss: local search -> federate to `k` peers -> frozen web corpus on miss -> cache locally -> optionally transfer to later peers when curator is “online.” Measure `Answer@k`, local-hit rate, federation lift over isolated peers, miss-to-future-hit conversion, unique artifact coverage, transfer cost, and diversity (`Gini`/entropy of retained records). Define **compounding** quantitatively as the area-under-curve improvement of local hit rate over time versus a no-sharing control, plus `MissToFutureHit@7d`: the fraction of misses that become hits for a different peer within 7 simulated days. This is runnable in a week on commodity hardware with `SQLite + FAISS` and a small embedding model such as `bge-small-en-v1.5`; however, the tradeoff is lower realism than a live network, so freeze the corpus and publish the simulator.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-5-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:329:5. **Q5**: The smallest viable 30-day launch is **one narrow wedge: agent-memory and coding-agent OSS**. Seed five named communities with about 20 contributors each: `aider`, `Continue`, `OpenHands`, `LangGraph`, and `Haystack`. Seed content should be concrete and already debated there: **LongMemEval** (arXiv:2410.10813, https://arxiv.org/abs/2410.10813), **LoCoMo** (arXiv:2402.17753, https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.17753), **Mem0** (arXiv:2504.19413, https://arxiv.org/abs/2504.19413; repo: https://github.com/mem0ai/mem0), **Agent Memory Benchmark** (https://agentmemorybenchmark.ai/), **FreshStack** (arXiv:2504.13128), and one federation substrate pair: `bluesky-social/atproto` (https://github.com/bluesky-social/atproto) and `holepunchto/hypercore` (https://github.com/holepunchto/hypercore). Timeline: week 1 seed 50-80 canonical artifacts and instrument miss/hit/provenance logs; week 2 recruit 25 design partners from those communities; week 3 run “ask-reuse-transfer” challenges and publish daily compounding graphs; week 4 ship a public writeup showing at least one concrete case where knowledge found by one contributor becomes a local hit for another. The upside is visible overlap fast; conversely, if you spread across unrelated OSS communities, you will get vanity signups without observable compounding.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-5-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:337:- **agentmemory**: strongest comparison on local retrieval quality; Akashik should position as “everything agentmemory does locally, plus cross-peer transfer and federation-level compounding.” Source: https://www.agentmemory.tech/
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-5-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:338:- **mem0**: strong comparison on production memory infrastructure; Akashik position is “peer-owned and federated memory commons, not an app-scoped memory layer.” Source: https://github.com/mem0ai/mem0 and arXiv:2504.19413.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-5-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:339:- **Bluesky / AT Protocol**: strongest comparison on federation architecture; Akashik position is “ATProto for knowledge artifacts and demand-shaped retrieval, not social posting.” Sources: https://github.com/bluesky-social/atproto and https://atproto.com/guides/overview
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-5-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:341:8. **Q8**: The devastating argument is: **“This is an overengineered federation story searching for a workload. Retrieval is already near-solved locally, cross-user query overlap is probably too low to create real compounding, and the moment you add federation you inherit abuse, provenance, privacy, deletion, and trust problems that centralized copilots or per-user memory systems avoid.”** The honest response is that this critique is valid unless Akashik proves a narrow, high-overlap use case quickly. So the burden of proof is not philosophical decentralization; it is a measurable federation lift in one community, with signed provenance, moderation/audit logs, and deletion/tombstone semantics good enough that the knowledge graph does not become an ungovernable exfiltration or misinformation cache. If the 30-day pilot cannot show repeated miss-to-hit conversion across different users, the project should be narrowed to local-first memory sync rather than a federated commons.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-5-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:343:If I were Sahar, the next engineering month I’d spend on **federation architecture** because that is the only part that creates a defensible product difference rather than another benchmark decimal. The next marketing/launch month I’d spend on **a 100-person pilot across aider, Continue, OpenHands, LangGraph, and Haystack** because those communities have overlapping questions and enough activity to make compounding visible fast. Specifically NOT **chasing `R@5` from `0.9202` toward `0.952` as the primary goal** because the upside is mostly narrative polish while the unresolved risk is whether cross-peer compounding exists at all.

./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-3.md:248:  - For Q3: a proposed benchmark for measuring federation-level
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-3.md:266:The next engineering month should go to federation architecture and federation measurement, not to squeezing the last `~2–4pp` out of LongMemEval-S `R@5`.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-3.md:269:- Your own data says LongMemEval-S is near a retrieval ceiling: `R@50 = 1.0`, `R@5 = 0.9202`, `NDCG@5 = 0.8836`, so pure rerank headroom is only about `4pp`. That is a marketing-number problem more than a product-identity problem.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-3.md:270:- LoCoMo says something different: `R@30 = 0.993` but `R@3 = 0.392`, so there is still real per-peer ranking work. However, that is still single-peer optimization, while the Akashik claim is federation compounding.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-3.md:274:- Spend `~70%` of the month on federation benchmark/instrumentation, room routing, caching, provenance, and trust.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-3.md:290:- AT Protocol is federated **server-to-server**, explicitly not device-level P2P ([AT overview](https://atproto.com/guides/overview)).
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-3.md:291:- IPFS/SSB replicate or route content, but they are not built around **semantic query-time federation + web-on-miss + signed local re-curation**.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-3.md:303:Yes, you should propose a benchmark. Existing federated benchmarks are the wrong shape.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-3.md:307:- FeB4RAG updates federated search for RAG, but still targets federated retrieval, not longitudinal knowledge compounding ([FeB4RAG](https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.11891)).
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-3.md:313:- Peers belong to overlapping rooms.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-3.md:320:- `Compounding Gain@t`: answer success with federation at time `t` minus isolated-peer success.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-3.md:331:- centralized shared index
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-3.md:332:- federation without caching
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-3.md:333:- federation with popularity-only caching
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-3.md:334:- federation with rarity-aware caching
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-3.md:349:- **Diversification blindness**: source selection can over-focus on the same dominant room/peer; diversification metrics were proposed for federated search years ago ([Hong & Luo Si](https://docs.lib.purdue.edu/ccpubs/543/)).
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-3.md:387:First rooms:
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-3.md:410:- Week 2: instrument rooms, provenance, and cache-hit analytics.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-3.md:412:- Week 4: publish day-30 numbers: web-miss reduction, propagated-answer rate, top contributing peers, top rooms.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-3.md:419:- Risk: the room converges on a few prolific curators.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-3.md:420:- Known mitigations: diversification penalties, per-author caps, source entropy metrics, MMR/`α-nDCG` style diversity ranking, and room-level quotas. Federated search literature explicitly studied novelty/diversification in source selection ([Hong & Luo Si](https://docs.lib.purdue.edu/ccpubs/543/)).
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-3.md:443:- For any enterprise use later, you will need deletion semantics, tombstones, key rotation/revocation, and room-level access controls.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-3.md:452:Closest **product** competitor. It is a shared knowledge commons of blocks/channels/connections, and public blocks accumulate context over time ([Are.na blocks](https://help.are.na/docs/getting-started/blocks), [API](https://www.are.na/developers)).  
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-3.md:454:Why it is not the same: central server, manual organization, no peer-local federation, no web-on-miss retrieval loop.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-3.md:457:Closest **protocol** cousin. It has signed personal repositories tied to DIDs and portable identity, but chooses server-hosted federation rather than end-device P2P ([AT repo](https://atproto.com/specs/repository), [overview](https://atproto.com/guides/overview)).  
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-3.md:458:Why it is close: signed user-owned records, federation, portability.  
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-3.md:486:1. federation benchmark harness
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-3.md:487:2. room-aware peer/resource selection

./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-3.md:798:      - Mastodon (federated social, no semantic graph)
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-3.md:803:      - Bluesky / AT Protocol (federated identity + content)
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-3.md:819:  - For Q3: a proposed benchmark for measuring federation-level
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-3.md:865:web search: federated search peer-to-peer information retrieval prior art paper semantic overlay network distributed IR ...
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-3.md:895:The next engineering month should go to federation architecture and federation measurement, not to squeezing the last `~2–4pp` out of LongMemEval-S `R@5`.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-3.md:898:- Your own data says LongMemEval-S is near a retrieval ceiling: `R@50 = 1.0`, `R@5 = 0.9202`, `NDCG@5 = 0.8836`, so pure rerank headroom is only about `4pp`. That is a marketing-number problem more than a product-identity problem.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-3.md:899:- LoCoMo says something different: `R@30 = 0.993` but `R@3 = 0.392`, so there is still real per-peer ranking work. However, that is still single-peer optimization, while the Akashik claim is federation compounding.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-3.md:903:- Spend `~70%` of the month on federation benchmark/instrumentation, room routing, caching, provenance, and trust.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-3.md:919:- AT Protocol is federated **server-to-server**, explicitly not device-level P2P ([AT overview](https://atproto.com/guides/overview)).
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-3.md:920:- IPFS/SSB replicate or route content, but they are not built around **semantic query-time federation + web-on-miss + signed local re-curation**.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-3.md:932:Yes, you should propose a benchmark. Existing federated benchmarks are the wrong shape.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-3.md:936:- FeB4RAG updates federated search for RAG, but still targets federated retrieval, not longitudinal knowledge compounding ([FeB4RAG](https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.11891)).
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-3.md:942:- Peers belong to overlapping rooms.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-3.md:949:- `Compounding Gain@t`: answer success with federation at time `t` minus isolated-peer success.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-3.md:960:- centralized shared index
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-3.md:961:- federation without caching
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-3.md:962:- federation with popularity-only caching
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-3.md:963:- federation with rarity-aware caching
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-3.md:978:- **Diversification blindness**: source selection can over-focus on the same dominant room/peer; diversification metrics were proposed for federated search years ago ([Hong & Luo Si](https://docs.lib.purdue.edu/ccpubs/543/)).
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-3.md:1016:First rooms:
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-3.md:1039:- Week 2: instrument rooms, provenance, and cache-hit analytics.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-3.md:1041:- Week 4: publish day-30 numbers: web-miss reduction, propagated-answer rate, top contributing peers, top rooms.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-3.md:1048:- Risk: the room converges on a few prolific curators.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-3.md:1049:- Known mitigations: diversification penalties, per-author caps, source entropy metrics, MMR/`α-nDCG` style diversity ranking, and room-level quotas. Federated search literature explicitly studied novelty/diversification in source selection ([Hong & Luo Si](https://docs.lib.purdue.edu/ccpubs/543/)).
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-3.md:1072:- For any enterprise use later, you will need deletion semantics, tombstones, key rotation/revocation, and room-level access controls.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-3.md:1081:Closest **product** competitor. It is a shared knowledge commons of blocks/channels/connections, and public blocks accumulate context over time ([Are.na blocks](https://help.are.na/docs/getting-started/blocks), [API](https://www.are.na/developers)).  
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-3.md:1083:Why it is not the same: central server, manual organization, no peer-local federation, no web-on-miss retrieval loop.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-3.md:1086:Closest **protocol** cousin. It has signed personal repositories tied to DIDs and portable identity, but chooses server-hosted federation rather than end-device P2P ([AT repo](https://atproto.com/specs/repository), [overview](https://atproto.com/guides/overview)).  
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-3.md:1087:Why it is close: signed user-owned records, federation, portability.

./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-3.md:1116:2. room-aware peer/resource selection

./docs/marketing/growth-sources-plan.md:21:| X/Twitter | API v2 (search) | `twitter_search` | Tweets mentioning keywords |
./docs/marketing/growth-sources-plan.md:33:Room keywords
./docs/marketing/growth-sources-plan.md:42:analyze graph → extract NEW keywords from indexed content

./docs/marketing/growth-sources-plan.md:56:Current `discover` is one-shot: it matches room keywords against a hardcoded list of known feeds. The discovery loop agent:
./docs/marketing/growth-sources-plan.md:58:1. **Extracts keywords from the graph itself** — after indexing ArXiv papers, it pulls out terms like "HNSW", "product quantization", "asymmetric search" that weren't in the original room keywords
./docs/marketing/growth-sources-plan.md:59:2. **Searches external APIs** — uses the extracted keywords to query the star-tracking sites, finding repos and tools the user didn't know about
./docs/marketing/growth-sources-plan.md:60:3. **Self-limits** — tracks which keyword→source pairs have already been explored, converges when no new sources are found
./docs/marketing/growth-sources-plan.md:68:discoveryLoop(deps)(room, opts) → ResultAsync<DiscoveryReport>
./docs/marketing/growth-sources-plan.md:69:  1. load room keywords
./docs/marketing/growth-sources-plan.md:72:  4. extract new keywords from freshly indexed content
./docs/marketing/growth-sources-plan.md:73:     (top TF-IDF terms not already in room keywords)
./docs/marketing/growth-sources-plan.md:74:  5. add new keywords to room
./docs/marketing/growth-sources-plan.md:83:                 index them, extract new keywords, discover more."
./docs/marketing/growth-sources-plan.md:89:akashik discover-loop --room homelab --max-iterations 3
./docs/marketing/growth-sources-plan.md:90:# iteration 1: found 4 sources from room keywords
./docs/marketing/growth-sources-plan.md:91:# iteration 2: found 2 more from extracted keywords ("VFIO", "iommu")
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-5-2026-05-26/probes/gemini-1.md:12:Akashik = federated knowledge commons for the OSS community.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-5-2026-05-26/probes/gemini-1.md:14:  local-first → federation-fan-out → web-on-miss → save-locally
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-5-2026-05-26/probes/gemini-1.md:22:  LoCoMo R@3 = 0.392, R@30 = 0.993 — 60pp rerank headroom unmined
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-5-2026-05-26/probes/gemini-1.md:34:     federation rather than per-peer retrieval?
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-5-2026-05-26/probes/gemini-1.md:44:Q3 — There is no public benchmark for federation-level
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-5-2026-05-26/probes/gemini-1.md:97:The next engineering month should be spent architecting the federation and its measurement infrastructure. The empirical data confirms the LME-S head is saturated (R@50=1.0, with only ~4pp NDCG@5 headroom). Chasing the final 2pp to match `agentmemory`'s 0.952 is optimizing for the obsolete "single-user agent memory" product frame. *The tradeoff:* Pausing local retrieval tuning leaves a massive 60pp of headroom on the table for the LoCoMo factual subset, meaning single-peer queries might still struggle with dense factual ranking. However, capturing that headroom requires large LLMs (like the broken gpt-oss:20b or regressing qwen2.5:7b) which introduces heavy latency or cloud dependencies, directly violating the local-first ethos required to build the network.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-5-2026-05-26/probes/gemini-1.md:108:We must propose a benchmark measuring longitudinal federation value.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-5-2026-05-26/probes/gemini-1.md:110:*   **Metrics:** `Web Avoidance Rate` (fraction of queries resolved by the federation without triggering web fetch) and `Propagation Half-Life` (median time until a newly acquired fact reaches 50% peer coverage).
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-5-2026-05-26/probes/gemini-1.md:116:2.  **Federated Diversification Blindness:** In federated search, retrieving the top 10 most relevant nodes often collapses to pulling from a single dominant peer, obscuring the loss of network diversity (Hong & Luo Si, TREC FedWeb 2013). *Fix:* Apply Maximal Marginal Relevance (MMR) or source-entropy penalties during the federation merge step.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-5-2026-05-26/probes/gemini-1.md:128:*   **The Signal:** Show the `web_fallback_rate` in the `rust` room dropping from ~90% on Day 1 to <40% by Day 30.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-5-2026-05-26/probes/gemini-1.md:133:*   **(a) Popularity cascade:** The graph collapses to a few highly active curators, suffocating diverse perspectives. *Solved by:* TREC FedWeb diversification penalties. *Mechanism:* Weight federation fan-out ordering by inverse document frequency across rooms, penalizing nodes that come from already heavily-sampled curators.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-5-2026-05-26/probes/gemini-1.md:135:*   **(c) Adversarial misinformation flood:** Plausible but malicious code/knowledge is injected. *Solved by:* AT Protocol (Bluesky) labelers and trust graphs. *Mechanism:* Confidence propagation with decay. Node confidence equals `author_reputation * original_confidence`. If a DID is flagged, the reputation score propagates downstream, automatically down-ranking the poisoned nodes across the federation.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-5-2026-05-26/probes/gemini-1.md:139:1.  **AT Protocol (Bluesky):** *Closest Protocol Analogue.* They solved portable, signed user-repositories via DIDs. *Positioning:* AT Protocol does federated microblogging via central relays; Akashik does query-driven semantic knowledge propagation via device-level P2P.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-5-2026-05-26/probes/gemini-1.md:147:*   **The Response:** The cold-start problem is real, but Akashik bypasses the "empty room" failure mode through its `web-on-miss` fallback. On Day 1, a user with zero peers still gets a highly functional, sub-second local agent memory tool that automates their web research. The federated compounding is strictly additive. The system provides immediate single-player utility to
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-5-2026-05-26/probes/gemini-1.md:153:The next engineering month should go to architecting the federation and its measurement infrastructure. 
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-5-2026-05-26/probes/gemini-1.md:154:**Rationale:** The core mission relies on the compounding loop, which cannot be measured or improved by single-peer retrieval metrics like LongMemEval-S R@5. Chasing R@5 is a marketing exercise for the old single-user product frame; building and measuring the federated mechanism directly validates Akashik's actual value proposition.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-5-2026-05-26/probes/gemini-1.md:177:- **Visible Compounding**: Track and publish the drop in `web_fallback_rate` for the `rust` room across the 30 days to prove network learning.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-5-2026-05-26/probes/gemini-1.md:180:- **(a) Popularity cascade** (The room converges on a few prolific curators). *Solved by:* TREC FedWeb resource selection; implemented via inverse document frequency weighting across rooms and source entropy diversification penalties.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-5-2026-05-26/probes/gemini-1.md:190:- **Argument**: "Akashik assumes that making knowledge portable and signed will naturally yield high-quality community memory. In reality, compounding requires network effects you haven't earned, and without density, the federated mechanism is slower and worse than a centralized web search. You have a coordination problem in an infrastructure costume."
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-5-2026-05-26/probes/gemini-1.md:191:- **Response**: "We grant that federation provides value only with density, but Akashik is designed to provide immediate single-player value before density is reached. Because the system gracefully falls back to a web search—and saves that result locally for the user's future sessions—a contributor with zero peers still gets a functional, accelerating local memory tool on day 1. Federation is strictly additive to this baseline, solving the cold-start problem by making the single-user experience worthwhile while the network effect builds."
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-5-2026-05-26/probes/gemini-1.md:194:If I were Sahar, the next engineering month I'd spend on building the AkashikBench-F simulation harness and instrumenting `web_fallback_rate` because these are the only metrics that actually measure the federated compounding claim. The next marketing/launch month I'd spend on onboarding 5-20 Rust infrastructure maintainers to seed a single, high-pain debugging room because deep coverage in one niche proves the mechanism better than thin coverage everywhere. Specifically NOT chasing the last 3% of R@5 on LongMemEval-S because that optimizes for the old, single-user product frame and distracts from the core peer-to-peer value proposition.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-6-2026-05-26/probes/gemini-probe-1.md:12:3. A fundamental pivot from 'akashik: agent memory product' to 'Akashik: federated knowledge commons for the OSS community'
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-6-2026-05-26/probes/gemini-probe-1.md:13:4. The articulation of the compounding mechanism (peer-local + federation-on-query + web-on-miss + save-locally + transfer-on-next-ask)

./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-6-2026-05-26/probes/gemini-probe-1.md:118:2. **Move the `.planning/` tree and `docs/research/` out of the working directory into `docs/archive/`**: They represent the old single-player memory product framing (RAG optimization / solo-dev GSD phases), which actively creates conflicting context for any OSS contributor trying to understand the new federated mission.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-6-2026-05-26/probes/gemini-probe-1.md:119:3. **Delete the experimental reranker modules (`cross-rerank.ts`, `llm-listwise-rerank.ts`, `binary-quantize.ts`)**: The empirical probes confirmed they offer no quality lift ("clean null", "below bi-encoder"). Removing them drastically simplifies `src/application/ask.ts` and allows engineering focus to shift entirely to federation stability (like fixing the hybrid remote search bug) rather than chasing SOTA embeddings.

./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-5-2026-05-26/probes/gemini-4.md:12:Akashik = federated knowledge commons for the OSS community.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-5-2026-05-26/probes/gemini-4.md:14:  local-first → federation-fan-out → web-on-miss → save-locally
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-5-2026-05-26/probes/gemini-4.md:22:  LoCoMo R@3 = 0.392, R@30 = 0.993 — 60pp rerank headroom unmined
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-5-2026-05-26/probes/gemini-4.md:34:     federation rather than per-peer retrieval?
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-5-2026-05-26/probes/gemini-4.md:44:Q3 — There is no public benchmark for federation-level
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-5-2026-05-26/probes/gemini-4.md:107:**Architecting the federation and its measurement infrastructure.** 
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-5-2026-05-26/probes/gemini-4.md:108:On the one hand, tuning per-peer `R@5` to match `agentmemory`'s 0.952 offers a recognizable marketing metric that early adopters easily understand. However, the severe tradeoff is that it completely ignores Akashik's core differentiator. Your own data shows LME-S head saturation (`R@50 = 1.0`), meaning single-peer optimization suffers diminishing returns. Conversely, building federation-level benchmarks validates the actual "knowledge commons" claim. Therefore, the month must be spent on federation routing and measurement (AkashikBench-F), even though it requires abandoning the easy `R@5` marketing race.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-5-2026-05-26/probes/gemini-4.md:114:### Q3 — Propose a benchmark for federation-level compounding
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-5-2026-05-26/probes/gemini-4.md:117:- **Simulation Protocol:** Deploy N=10 peer simulators in overlapping rooms. Run a stream of 500 time-ordered queries (incorporating a realistic 20% peer churn/offline rate). On a federation miss, the peer accesses a central 'oracle' (simulated web search), saves the result locally, and subsequent queries test network propagation.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-5-2026-05-26/probes/gemini-4.md:118:- **Metrics:** `Web Avoidance Rate` (fraction of queries resolved without the oracle over time) and `Compounding Gain@t` (federated answer success minus isolated-peer success).
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-5-2026-05-26/probes/gemini-4.md:123:2. **Availability Confounding:** Evaluating federated retrieval with all peers always online vastly overstates performance compared to real P2P networks; **fix this** by introducing a realistic probabilistic churn model during benchmark simulation.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-5-2026-05-26/probes/gemini-4.md:124:3. **Corpus Contamination:** Benchmarking federation with overlapping seed corpora makes basic deduplication look like genuine knowledge transfer; **fix this** by strictly enforcing disjoint initial seeding, which accurately isolates true cross-peer discovery for the long tail of niche queries.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-5-2026-05-26/probes/gemini-4.md:133:- **(a) Popularity Cascade:** A known solution is **Diversity Ranking (TREC FedWeb 2013)**, which weights federation fan-out by inverse document frequency across rooms to ensure source entropy; however, the tradeoff is that diversity penalties can occasionally suppress the genuinely most accurate popular answer.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-5-2026-05-26/probes/gemini-4.md:138:- **Are.na**: The closest *product* competitor (centralized curation/attribution). Are.na provides a frictionless centralized UX with strong community features; however, Akashik supersedes it by replacing central servers with peer-local federation, prioritizing data sovereignty over immediate onboarding ease.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-5-2026-05-26/probes/gemini-4.md:143:**Counter-argument:** "This is a coordination problem in an infrastructure costume. The federated compounding mechanism is architecturally sound but practically useless without critical mass. Until network density is achieved, users will experience a slower, worse version of web search, leading to churn before compounding materializes."
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-5-2026-05-26/probes/gemini-4.md:144:**Honest Response:** "We acknowledge that federation value is strictly additive and dependent on density. However, Akashik solves the cold-start problem by providing strict single-player value on day one: the local-first web-fallback loop ensures that even a user with zero peers gets a functional, accumulating local knowledge base. The tradeoff is that early adopters bear the cost of local indexing, but they retain sovereign ownership of their data, and the network effect becomes a progressive enhancement rather than a hard prerequisite."
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-5-2026-05-26/probes/gemini-4.md:148:If I were Sahar, the next engineering month I'd spend on building the AkashikBench-F federation measurement harness and assessing AT Protocol DID compatibility because the project's core claim of monotonic compounding currently lacks empirical proof. The next marketing/launch month I'd spend on seeding the Rust OSS community with 5 'librarians' and 95 early adopters because demonstrating a sharp drop in the `web_fallback_rate` within a single, high-density domain is the only way to prove the architecture works in practice. Specifically NOT tuning the LongMemEval-S R@5 metric because competing on single-peer memory benchmarks completely abandons Akashik's federated differentiator and chases a mathematical ceiling we've already hit.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:13:"Akashik: federated knowledge commons for the open-source community as a whole."

./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:41:  STEP 2: Federation fan-out → connected peers in shared rooms answer

./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:172:    federation rather than tuning per-peer retrieval)? Where should

./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:183:    federated peer network compound knowledge over time?" The
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:194:      (b) federated retrieval evaluation
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:204:    rooms do they share, and what content do they save such that
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:215:      (c) adversarial contributors flood the federation with
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:222:    federated commons. Who are the REAL existing systems that
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:227:      - Mastodon (federated social, no semantic graph)
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:232:      - Bluesky / AT Protocol (federated identity + content)
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:248:  - For Q3: a proposed benchmark for measuring federation-level
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:265:`LongMemEval-S` is telling you the headline number is near its ceiling: `R@50 = 1.0`, `R@5 = 0.9202`, `NDCG@5 = 0.8836`, so the remaining room is mostly intra-top-5 ordering, not missing evidence. That makes further gains partly a marketing question if the goal is leaderboard optics against `agentmemory`, not the highest-leverage engineering question. On the other hand, `LoCoMo` still shows a real ranking problem (`R@30 = 0.993` but `R@3 = 0.392`), so abandoning retrieval work entirely would be premature. The tradeoff is simple: spend maybe `10-20%` of the month on low-risk per-peer improvements like temporal gating, better segmentation, and stronger reranker evaluation; spend the other `80-90%` on federation architecture, compounding instrumentation, and launch-loop proof, because that is where Akashik is differentiated.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:279:- **Process**: queries arrive over simulated days. A peer answers locally, then via federation, then via web-on-miss, then caches locally with attribution.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:293:  - Include adversarial inserts and duplicate-heavy hot-topic rooms.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:307:- Resource selection and result merging are separate problems; TREC federated search literature treats them separately for a reason.[5]
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:309:- Duplicate evidence across peers can inflate apparent federation gains.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:310:- Privacy/compliance blind spots: if rooms carry logs, stack traces, or emails, GDPR/PII handling matters even for OSS-adjacent use.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:325:- **First 100 contributors**: target `4 rooms x 25 people`.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:331:- **Why these rooms**: they have constant repeated debugging, public artifacts, and frequent “I solved this yesterday” moments. That is ideal for visible compounding.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:341:  - Week 2: instrument federation metrics, especially web-miss rate and transfer rate.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:343:  - Week 4: onboard fresh users into the same rooms and measure whether they hit community answers before the web.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:357:- Recommendation: room-level opt-in pinning, replica floors for “rare but validated” items, and maintainer/archivist peers.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:368:- **Are.na**: closest behavioral competitor. People already use it as a shared research memory, but it is centralized and manual rather than federated and retrieval-native.[8]
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:369:- **Bluesky / AT Protocol**: closest federation-governance counterpart. It is relevant for identity, moderation, labels, and interoperability, even though it is not a knowledge graph.[4]
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:374:**Counter-argument:** “The whole project assumes people will contribute enough high-quality, legally shareable, evidence-backed knowledge for the commons to improve over time. In practice, most saved notes are noisy, duplicative, stale, or private. Without a central editor, the network compounds junk faster than truth, and the result is a decentralized landfill rather than a collective memory.”
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:376:**Response:** Akashik should not claim that every saved note becomes durable truth. The system works only if it compounds **attributed, evidence-backed, query-reused** knowledge. That is why the product must privilege provenance, room-scoped trust, replayable evidence, soft moderation labels, and visible transfer events over raw note volume. The point is not “everything anyone saves becomes the record”; it is “the subset of community work that repeatedly proves useful becomes easier to find, reuse, and attribute without requiring a central owner.”
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:379:The next engineering month should be spent proving the **federation thesis**, not polishing the single-peer benchmark headline. Concretely: build the compounding benchmark and simulator; instrument `web-miss`, `transfer`, `churn`, `attribution`, and `diversity`; ship room-level caching/pinning and provenance primitives; and run a narrow 100-user launch in high-overlap OSS rooms. Keep a smaller retrieval lane for `E10`, segmentation, and better reranker eval on `LoCoMo`, because local ranking still matters. However, the core risk is no longer “can we beat `0.925` on `R@5`?” It is “can we show that knowledge acquired by one contributor becomes faster to retrieve for another contributor over time, without centralization and without the commons collapsing into noise?” That is the month’s real work.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:451:"Akashik: federated knowledge commons for the open-source community as a whole."
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:454:a shared, contributor-owned memory substrate where every piece of
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:470:  - what it researched on the web when the federation couldn't answer
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:479:  STEP 2: Federation fan-out → connected peers in shared rooms answer
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:481:  STEP 3: If federation can't satisfy → harness reaches the web on
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:485:  STEP 5: Next user B asks similar question → federation fan-out

./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:632:      (b) federated retrieval evaluation

./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:653:      (c) adversarial contributors flood the federation with

./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:665:      - Mastodon (federated social, no semantic graph)
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:670:      - Bluesky / AT Protocol (federated identity + content)
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:686:  - For Q3: a proposed benchmark for measuring federation-level
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:730:I’m treating this as a research synthesis, not a code task. First step is establishing prior art and evaluation literature around local-first/P2P knowledge systems, federated retrieval, and benchmark design so the recommendations are anchored to real systems rather than analogy.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:747:`LongMemEval-S` is telling you the headline number is near its ceiling: `R@50 = 1.0`, `R@5 = 0.9202`, `NDCG@5 = 0.8836`, so the remaining room is mostly intra-top-5 ordering, not missing evidence. That makes further gains partly a marketing question if the goal is leaderboard optics against `agentmemory`, not the highest-leverage engineering question. On the other hand, `LoCoMo` still shows a real ranking problem (`R@30 = 0.993` but `R@3 = 0.392`), so abandoning retrieval work entirely would be premature. The tradeoff is simple: spend maybe `10-20%` of the month on low-risk per-peer improvements like temporal gating, better segmentation, and stronger reranker evaluation; spend the other `80-90%` on federation architecture, compounding instrumentation, and launch-loop proof, because that is where Akashik is differentiated.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:761:- **Process**: queries arrive over simulated days. A peer answers locally, then via federation, then via web-on-miss, then caches locally with attribution.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:775:  - Include adversarial inserts and duplicate-heavy hot-topic rooms.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:789:- Resource selection and result merging are separate problems; TREC federated search literature treats them separately for a reason.[5]
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:791:- Duplicate evidence across peers can inflate apparent federation gains.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:792:- Privacy/compliance blind spots: if rooms carry logs, stack traces, or emails, GDPR/PII handling matters even for OSS-adjacent use.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:807:- **First 100 contributors**: target `4 rooms x 25 people`.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:813:- **Why these rooms**: they have constant repeated debugging, public artifacts, and frequent “I solved this yesterday” moments. That is ideal for visible compounding.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:823:  - Week 2: instrument federation metrics, especially web-miss rate and transfer rate.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:825:  - Week 4: onboard fresh users into the same rooms and measure whether they hit community answers before the web.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:839:- Recommendation: room-level opt-in pinning, replica floors for “rare but validated” items, and maintainer/archivist peers.
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:850:- **Are.na**: closest behavioral competitor. People already use it as a shared research memory, but it is centralized and manual rather than federated and retrieval-native.[8]

In practice, most saved notes are noisy, duplicative, stale, or private. Without a central editor, the network compounds junk faster than truth, and the result is a decentralized landfill rather than a collective memory.”
./docs/research/octopus-discover/round-4-2026-05-26/probes/codex-0.md:858:**Response:** Akashik should not claim that every saved note becomes durable truth. The system works only if it compounds **attributed, evidence-backed, query-reused** knowledge. That is why the product must privilege provenance, room-scoped trust, replayable evidence, soft moderation labels, and visible transfer events over raw note volume. The point is not “everything anyone saves becomes the record”; it is “the subset of community work that repeatedly proves useful becomes easier to find, reuse, and attribute without requiring a central owner.”

Concretely: build the compounding benchmark and simulator; instrument `web-miss`, `transfer`, `churn`, `attribution`, and `diversity`; ship room-level caching/pinning and provenance primitives; and run a narrow 100-user launch in high-overlap OSS rooms. Keep a smaller retrieval lane for `E10`, segmentation, and better reranker eval on `LoCoMo`, because local ranking still matters. However, the core risk is no longer “can we beat `0.925` on `R@5`?” It is “can we show that knowledge acquired by one contributor becomes faster to retrieve for another contributor over time, without centralization and without the commons collapsing into noise?” That is the month’s real work.
